title: Dr. Holly Wood: Why Pleasure Is Self-Care for Moms, Not Selfishness
guestBio: Dr. Holly Wood is a sexologist, sex therapist, and women's sexual wellness advocate dedicated to helping women reclaim their sexuality and dismantle shame. She specializes in helping women prioritize pleasure as part of a healthy, fulfilling life.
excerpt: Sexologist Dr. Holly Wood joins Aimee to discuss why pleasure isn't selfish—it's self-care. Learn how to reclaim your sexuality, communicate with your partner, and navigate desire through motherhood.
metaDescription: Sexologist Dr. Holly Wood shares how mothers can reclaim sexuality, communicate needs, and prioritize pleasure as self-care on The Manage Her podcast.
keyTakeaways: Pleasure is not selfish; it's a vital form of self-care that women deserve to prioritize in their lives.
Motherhood naturally changes your relationship with your body and desire, and accepting this evolution is part of the journey.
Open communication with your partner about your needs and desires is essential for maintaining intimacy through life's seasons.
Understanding that women's desire works differently than men's can help normalize your experience and reduce shame.
Creating safe spaces for pleasure in your relationship requires intentional effort from both partners.
Dismantling shame around sexuality is an ongoing process that benefits your overall well-being.
You have the right to pursue the sex and pleasure you desire, regardless of your life stage or circumstances.

timestamps.label: The Journey to Becoming a Sexologist
Motherhood's Impact on Body and Sexuality
The Importance of Self-Care and Pleasure

content:
Let's talk about something that might make you squirm a little in your seat. When was the last time you thought about pleasure—not just sexual pleasure, but any kind of pleasure—as something you deserved? Not earned. Not justified. Simply deserved.

If you're struggling to answer that question, you're not alone. In this powerful conversation, Aimee sits down with sexologist Dr. Holly Wood to tackle one of the most taboo yet essential topics for women: reclaiming our right to pleasure, especially as mothers navigating the complex terrain of desire, identity, and intimacy.

The Revolutionary Idea That Pleasure Is Self-Care

Dr. Holly Wood didn't set out to become a sexologist, but her journey into this field reflects something profound about how women are conditioned to think about sexuality and pleasure. Her work centers on a revolutionary premise: pleasure isn't selfish—it's self-care. This isn't just a catchy phrase; it's a fundamental shift in how we approach our well-being as women.

Think about it. You wouldn't feel guilty about getting enough sleep or eating nutritious food. You understand these as basic needs for your health. Yet when it comes to sexual pleasure and intimacy, many of us carry a burden of shame that keeps us disconnected from this vital aspect of our humanity.

Dr. Wood emphasizes that sexuality is an integral part of who we are as humans. It's not something separate from your identity as a mother, leader, or partner—it's woven into the fabric of your being. When you neglect this part of yourself, you're not just missing out on physical pleasure; you're disconnecting from a fundamental aspect of your identity.

Motherhood Changes Everything (And That's Okay)

One of the most honest parts of this conversation centers on how motherhood impacts our relationship with our bodies and our desire. If you've become a mother and felt like your sexuality disappeared overnight, Dr. Wood wants you to know that your experience is completely normal.

Pregnancy, childbirth, breastfeeding, sleep deprivation, and the monumental shift in identity that comes with becoming a mother—all of these factors naturally affect your connection to your body and your desire. The key isn't to fight against these changes or to rush back to who you were before. It's to accept that you're evolving and to approach your sexuality with curiosity rather than judgment.

Early motherhood, in particular, presents unique challenges for maintaining intimacy. Dr. Wood offers practical perspective here: yes, quickies count. The idea that every intimate encounter needs to be a lengthy, elaborate affair is another myth that keeps women feeling inadequate. Sometimes connection happens in stolen moments, and that's not only okay—it's often the reality of this season.

Understanding How Your Desire Actually Works

Here's something that might surprise you: women's desire often works differently than the narrative we've been sold. Many women experience what's called responsive desire, meaning their interest in intimacy builds in response to physical touch and emotional connection rather than appearing spontaneously.

This doesn't mean there's anything wrong with you if you don't feel a constant, burning desire. It means you need to understand your own patterns and communicate them to your partner. It also means creating space for desire to emerge rather than expecting it to appear on command.

Dr. Wood emphasizes that understanding these differences can be incredibly liberating. When you stop measuring your sexuality against unrealistic standards, you can start exploring what actually works for you and your relationship.

The Art of Communication in Intimacy

One of the most practical aspects of this conversation focuses on how to communicate your needs to your partner. This isn't just about asking for what you want in the bedroom—it's about creating ongoing dialogue about intimacy, connection, and the changes you're experiencing.

Open communication requires vulnerability from both partners. It means being willing to share when you're struggling, when something isn't working, or when you need something different. It also means creating a safe space where your partner can do the same.

Dr. Wood emphasizes that this communication isn't a one-time conversation. Your needs and desires will continue to evolve throughout your life, which means your communication needs to be ongoing as well. The couple that can navigate these conversations with openness and curiosity is the couple that can maintain intimacy through all of life's seasons.

Creating Safe Spaces for Pleasure

Creating space for pleasure in your relationship requires intentional effort. This goes beyond scheduling date nights or buying lingerie. It's about cultivating an environment where both partners feel safe to be vulnerable, to express their needs, and to explore their desires without judgment.

Safety in intimacy means emotional safety as much as physical safety. It means knowing that you can communicate a boundary and have it respected. It means feeling accepted in your body as it is right now, not as it was or as you think it should be.

This kind of safety doesn't happen overnight. It's built through consistent, caring interactions over time. It requires both partners to show up with empathy and to prioritize the relationship even when life gets chaotic.

Permission to Prioritize Your Pleasure

Perhaps the most powerful message in this conversation is the permission Dr. Wood gives women to pursue the sex and pleasure they deserve. This isn't about being selfish or neglecting your responsibilities. It's about recognizing that your well-being matters and that sexual wellness is part of overall wellness.

You don't have to earn the right to pleasure through perfect behavior or by meeting everyone else's needs first. You don't have to wait until your body looks a certain way or until life becomes less complicated. You deserve pleasure and intimacy right now, in whatever season of life you're in.

This conversation is the permission slip you've been waiting for. It's time to dismantle the shame, normalize pleasure, and reclaim this vital part of yourself. Your sexuality isn't separate from your identity as a leader, mother, or partner—it's part of what makes you whole.

quiz.title: What's Your Relationship with Pleasure and Self-Care?
quiz.description: Discover how you currently approach pleasure, intimacy, and self-care in your life. This quiz will help you understand your unique patterns and what might be holding you back from the fulfillment you deserve.

questions.question: When you think about taking time for activities that bring you pure joy or pleasure, what's your first instinct?

options.text: I feel a twinge of guilt and start mentally listing everything else I should be doing instead
I want to, but I'm not always sure what would actually bring me pleasure anymore
I try to schedule it in, but life often gets in the way and I struggle to follow through
I remind myself that this is essential for my well-being and make it happen

questions.question: How do you typically approach conversations about intimacy and sexual needs with your partner?

options.text: I drop hints and hope they pick up on what I need rather than being direct
I want to communicate more openly but feel awkward or don't know how to start
I try to bring things up but we often get interrupted or the timing never feels right
I'm learning to be more direct about my needs, even when it feels uncomfortable

questions.question: If your relationship with your body and desire has changed (due to motherhood, age, or life circumstances), how do you feel about that?

options.text: I worry that something is wrong with me and that I should get back to how I used to be
I feel confused and disconnected, like I don't recognize this version of myself
I'm working on accepting the changes but it's a daily struggle some days
I'm learning to embrace this evolution as a natural part of my journey

questions.question: When it comes to shame around sexuality or pleasure, where are you in your journey?

options.text: I carry a lot of shame but rarely talk about it or examine where it comes from
I know shame is affecting me, but I'm just beginning to understand how deeply
I'm actively working on it but find myself slipping back into old patterns sometimes
I've done significant work to dismantle shame and help others do the same

questions.question: How do you prioritize your own pleasure and desires in your daily life?

options.text: I tend to focus on everyone else's needs first and hope there's time left over for me
I want to prioritize myself more but I'm not always clear on what I actually want
I'm working on creating better boundaries but consistency is my biggest challenge
I've learned that prioritizing my pleasure makes me a better partner, mother, and leader

questions.question: What's your relationship with the idea that 'pleasure is self-care, not selfishness'?

options.text: Intellectually I get it, but emotionally I still feel selfish when I prioritize pleasure
It's a new concept for me and I'm curious but not sure how to apply it practically
I believe it's true and I'm working on making choices that reflect this belief
I fully embrace this philosophy and try to model it for other women in my life

results.title: The Permission Seeker
results.description: You know pleasure and self-care are important, but you struggle with feeling like you've 'earned' the right to prioritize them. You often find yourself justifying your needs or waiting for others to give you permission to pursue what brings you joy. Your caring nature sometimes means you put everyone else first, leaving little energy for yourself. This episode will help you understand that pleasure isn't selfish—it's essential self-care that you deserve simply by being human.

results.title: The Quiet Explorer
results.description: You're curious about deepening your relationship with pleasure and intimacy, but you prefer to process things privately before taking action. You might feel like you're 'behind' or don't know where to start, especially if motherhood or life changes have shifted your relationship with your body and desires. Your thoughtful approach is actually a strength. Dr. Holly Wood's insights will give you the framework and validation you need to begin exploring what you truly want without judgment.

results.title: The Intentional Builder
results.description: You understand that pleasure and intimacy require intentional effort, and you're willing to do the work to create positive change in your life. You might be navigating communication challenges with your partner or working through your own relationship with shame around sexuality. Your commitment to growth is admirable, and you're ready for practical strategies. This episode will provide you with expert guidance on how to build the safe spaces and open communication that healthy pleasure requires.

results.title: The Empowered Advocate
results.description: You've already begun your journey of reclaiming pleasure as a vital part of your well-being, and you're passionate about helping other women do the same. You understand that dismantling shame is ongoing work and that every season of life brings new challenges to navigate. Your leadership in this area is valuable, but even advocates need support and fresh perspectives. Dr. Wood's expertise will deepen your understanding and give you new tools to continue your own evolution while supporting others.

transcript: [music] Hi everyone and welcome back to the manage her, the podcast where we turn invisible work of women into powerful intentional leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickbus, author of the manage her entrepreneur and mama of six. Today's episode's going to be a little racier than usual, and honestly, I'm here for it because managing our lives as women also means managing our bodies, our energy, and yes, our pleasure. My guest today is Dr. Holly Wood. And yes, that is her real name. Known online as the Hollywood sexologist, she's a wife, mom of two young children, a 5-month-old and a 2-year-old, and she holds a PhD in human sexuality. Holly is passionate about helping women and couples understand intimacy through a lens of self-awareness, education, and empowerment. Her work focuses on dismantling shame, normalizing pleasure, and helping women reconnect with their sensual selves, especially during the demanding seasons of motherhood and partnership. You can find her at the hollywoodsexologist.com or on Instagram, Dr. her Hollywood PhD and on YouTube at the Hollywood Sexyologist. And trust me, her content is refreshingly real. You're going to love it. So, settle in. This conversation is about reclaiming one of the most important parts of being a woman, your connection to your body, your desire, and your truth. Welcome, Holly. Yay. It's so nice to have you on the show, honey. Thank you so much. >> Of course. Thank you so much for having me. >> Yeah. So, let's start with your name, Dr. Hollywood. That's unforgettable. How was having a name like that shape your brand or the way you approach your work? >> Yeah. Yeah. So, I think at first, so um Holly was actually my middle name and is now my first name. Wood has always been my last name. I was my maiden name. And I think at first I was a little bit nervous about it coming off as unprofessional or kind of cheesy. But then part of me was like, well, I think it catches attention. and also my professionalism and my expertise will shine through beyond my name. So, I kept it. And now that I'm married, I was kind enough to uh adopt a hyphen for my husband, but I do still practice with the last name Wood because I really like my name. >> It's really cute and it definitely sticks with you. So, you've got a PhD in human sexuality, but you're also a wife and a mom to two very young kids. What drew you into this field and how did you know it was your calling? >> Yes, great question. So, I started out in my undergraduate career going to college studying premed and about halfway through I was just getting really burnt out by the amount of chemistry and stuff that wasn't really sparking my interest. And so, I started taking gender and sexuality classes as a breath requirement and I was like, "Oh, I actually really really like this stuff." So that combined with um unfortunately my own experiences as a survivor of sexual violence throughout college um kind of propelled me to start volunteering at my local rape crisis center. And so I was doing rape crisis work in which I would show up to uh hospitals when survivors reported a sexual assault and they would go to get a SAR exam or what's also known as a rape kit done. I would show up as an advocate and a support person and someone to provide them with resources during this really really difficult scary part of their lives. Um so that happened and then in order to have two jobs. I had one job during the daytime. Um I was going to school. I also had a job doing retail. Um always hustling. And then a job at nighttime in order to have a second job had to be overnight. So I started moonlighting at the adult toy store. So [laughter] full circle and that was my fun stuff. So I did like the really hard trauma work doing the rape crisis work and then going to work at the adult toy store was the fun part, right? I got to talk with couples and women about exploring their bodies and vibrators and dildos and all that fun stuff. And so then I was like, this is what I want to do, right? drawing from my own experience in sexual violence and rediscovering pleasure post-t trauma, I was like, I want to work with survivors engaging in pleasurable consensual sex again because I saw such a gap between reduction of PTSD symptoms and engaging in pleasure. So, that's how I knew like this is what I want to do. I want to be a sex therapist specifically to help survivors of sexual violence, but also working with the whole gamut of sex therapy. >> I love it. what's such such good and such important work. You know, another example of women supporting women out of a need and something that you experienced in your life. I see that a lot on this show when people are the experiences that we go through in our lifetime, in our human lives kind of lead us down the path that we're supposed to take. It seems like and uh you see that. >> So many of us grew up with mixed messages, curiosity, shame, silence, stigma around sexuality. How do you help women start unlearning those messages and come back to their authentic selves? >> I love this. One of the very first homework assignments I give clients when they come into my office is called the my sex essay. And it really just encourages you to go back through your sexual history and your upbringing and think about where some of these messages came from. Right? some of them. The first question in this my sex essay exploration is what did you learn from your family, your culture, and your religious upbringing about sex? And people will start to go, "Oh my gosh, I didn't really think about how much, you know, going to Catholic school, even though I'm not Catholic anymore." Like going to Catholic school really ingrained this idea that sex was shameful or um even I heard earlier today, you know, the message was any type of sex that was outside of marriage and not for making babies was bad. And so really get diving into where did these messages come from? What are some of the earlier sexual experiences or maybe things we've heard about our bodies, right, to cover up or you shouldn't dress like this or good girls don't want sex, right? That's something that bad girls do. So, really diving into where did these messages come from? What are some of the first messages you learned about sex, your body, masturbation, relationships? So, that way you can see where they came from. And then also, do these messages still fit? Is this something that aligns with how you feel today? >> Absolutely. That's a great place to start for sure. I love that. I'm like thinking about myself. Oh my gosh, there so much to crack into. [laughter] >> I was lucky that my mom was kind of a feminist, but she's also a Christian. So, there was a little bit of a balance in the house with it. You know, there was the biology part of sex that she was very open in our house. And but then there were there were some aspects that were a little you know a little shoddy. I mean through my teenage years there were some years there where you know there was a little bit of a a little shaming for how I was feeling. But I think that as I grew into adulthood and especially motherhood kind of allowed me to claim my sexuality and my femininity in a way that I don't think I it was quite unclaimed before. And I think um you know all my kids were born at home and I feel like there was something about the experience of having my kids in such a raw and kind of it's honestly it's kind of sexual when you have your kids unmedicated. There's like a real it's a very transitional and transformative experience and it really felt like a full circle experience for me from like you know from conception to birth actually felt very complete and very like oh I get it now >> like I get this process. So you've been open about navigating early motherhood while maintaining intimacy and identity. How has becoming a mom changed your relationship with your body and your sexuality? >> Very good questions. with relationship to my body. So, I think it's also important background information that I do have two stepchildren or whom I call my bonus children. Um, one of them is my daughter, my stepdaughter. She's been in my life since she was 15. We lived together since she was 15. That was my first little taste of motherhood and being aware of how I treat my body with a young girl in the house, especially coming from a home in which my own mother didn't treat her own body very nicely. And I know how much that impacted me. And that was something that I wanted to be very sure I wasn't going to replicate or model to my stepdaughter whom I care about very much. And so now even as having my own biological children, I know it sounds so silly, but I've always been so insecure about my nose and um like I I don't know why it's bo. It's just it's bothered me, right? We all have our little things. And so I was so insecure about my nose and then having two little girls, I'm like, "Oh my goodness, you know, they might have my nose, right?" And it's like, why would I? But I see it on their little faces and I'm like, "Oh my gosh, they have the cutest little noses." is like, how could I not like my own nose when I see how beautiful it looks on my daughter? So, I think that's something that is can be really healing about being a mother who has struggled with body image um and how we feel about ourselves to see that in our children whom we think are the most beautiful beings to ever exist because they're our kids, right? That can be incredibly healing. And then the sexuality piece, again, going back to my step-daughter, it's been really fun and interesting to have open conversations with her because she was a teenager, right? And so I was the point person for having those conversations as she was coming into that stage of life. Um, which I think is really important, too, because not everybody has maybe these traumatic stories or messages about sex being bad, but a lot of us grow up in homes where it's just not talked about at all. So there's almost like an implied shame around anything sexual with what is not said. And so it's made me be very intentional about having open dialogue and being like I'm the I'm the safest, most educated person that my child can talk to about sex. So I would rather them come to me than anybody else. And so making sure that I have a home in which that is the culture of the home that they can come to me about those things, that's been really important. And then the other like relationship I guess with sexuality is just how to prioritize that part of our relationships when we are in such a busy sleepdeprived stage of life. Oh my gosh. Yeah. Especially cuz you're all touched out. You know, they're holding babies all day and they're all over your breasts and you don't feel like you own your body anymore. You know, it's definitely being owned by this little tiny person. So, how do you overcome that? There's a few things that I find to be helpful. Um, one of which is to separate mother from like who I am as a sexual being. I think for a lot of us those feel even though we we can absolutely be both. We are both as women. But I think for a lot of us it's so hard to switch from mom mode to being like a sexual being again. So, I think having transitions like you know what I when I'm walking around the house in my nursing bra and my sweatpants, like that's my like, you know, my postpartum mom outfit that is certainly functional, but it doesn't make me feel sexy. So, like, how can I transition into an outfit in which I feel more aligned with that sexual identity of mine? or even if I'm feeling touched out, you know, is there a ritual that we can incorporate that's like I'm going to take a shower and I'm going to rinse away the feeling of my baby clinging to my breast and the switch into allowing my body to be a playground for myself and for my husband. >> I love that. I love that so much. That's such good advice cuz I feel like we as moms, everybody's gone through this. This is not like something that, you know, uncommon. It's something that we all kind of have to reinvent the wheel when we go through this. And I'd love for women to have these conversations with each other a little bit more. I'm so glad we get to have this conversation today. Um that it will be out there in Evergreen, that people can hear us talk about how to get through because it's hard. It is hard to make the transition, especially when they're little, you know, [clears throat] um surviving, you know, just surviving that transition into motherhood with your sexuality intact. Yeah. Yeah. And I mean, embracing the quickie is I think so helpful and important. Like it's a small touch point to like continue to engage in intimacy and prioritize intimacy. And I when I say intimacy, I don't just mean sex, right? But prioritize that emotional, physical connection with your partner that we need to maintain because at some point our kids are going to eventually leave and I'm going to be stuck with just this man again. So, how do I continue to create those touch points for intimacy? And man, if both kids are asleep at the same time that could be a signal for game on if we're both up for it. Heck yeah. I know. We put our kids down to bed. Our kids have a pretty solid sleeping schedule. And there's like a nice I just we set aside a little there's a little window of time. I'd say it's about an hour a night between like 9 and 10. You know, we might be open for business. It's negotiable. I think it's nice when you have those kinds of like defined boundaries so that your spouse knows that there is a place for it, you know, in the day. There's a place in the day where there's time for it. >> Right. Right. And having that boundary like stated ahead of time that there is a time and there is a place for it. Because when I am like bent over in the bottle drawer, I don't need my husband grinding up against me. that's just going to frustrate me even more when I'm overworked, underslept, and touched out. But there are times when I'm it's much more likely that there's going to be a green light or when it is negotiable. >> Well, I love what you you and I were talking about how you guys have an actual designated space, too. And that's another way to do it. [laughter] >> Yes. Yes. Especially, I mean, right now with with my little one being 5 months, she sleeps in a bassinet in our bedroom. And so, if you have if you have a guest room or an extra space in your house, I love having that as like the designated spot to like shift out from this place where I'm folding laundry or where my baby is sleeping. Especially our bedroom, right, is what we normally define as like the space for sex. But if the baby's in there, that's not just a space for sex. That's a space where I wake up at 3:00 a.m. to feed her, right? That's the space where I'm folding laundry in the bed. And so, are there other places in the home that can be more sexy, like on top of the kitchen counter or in the office when it feels like, you know, kind of like a sneaking away to a hidden sexy spot. >> I love that. That's such good advice. It really is. And I feel like we have to honor our husbands and make ourselves, you know, creating those kind having that kind of communication with them where they know that there's a place there's a time, you know, that they're going to be able to get a piece of us cuz they they miss us. >> You know, our babies take a lot of >> honoring ourselves. >> Yeah. Absolutely. Heck yeah. That's right. Showing up for us too and our sexuality and our pleasure, right? >> Yeah. So, there's a common story that once women have kids, their desire disappears. What's actually going on biologically and emotionally in that season? Oh, goodness. Well, if you've been through motherhood, you know that it is certainly an emotional roller coaster. So, emotionally, it takes quite a long time for you to regulate. And biologically, hormonally, you're going through all these huge hormonal shifts that allow you to create a baby, to feed a baby. And so, physically, one thing that I think is important for folks to know that I don't think gets talked about a lot is the impact of the hormonal shifts on your actual sexual organs. So, once you have a baby, your body is being pumped with prolactin so that way you can produce milk, right? and you then produce milk to feed the baby and in order to have prolapin there's a suppression of estrogen and when estrogen is suppressed you can cause uh vaginal dryness and it almost replicates what happens when you go into menopause right when there's a reduction in estrogen production so knowing that I think is important so we can prepare for discomfort like there can be physical discomfort when you're having dried tissue down there um so being prepared for that and knowing what questions to ask and how you can talk to your doctor about it, right? Just like some topical estrogen can make all the difference or some lubricant can make all the difference so that it's actually comfortable so you'll want to engage in sex because most of us don't want to engage in sex if it's going to be painful. True. Absolutely. I love that. Yeah. So, for the moms who are listening who are feeling disconnected from their sexual selves, where's a safe and compassionate place for them to start? I think a a really good place to start is just honoring pleasure, whatever that looks like, either inside the bedroom or outside the bedroom. I think because that transition into motherhood, you're so much giving yourself to other people, whether it's your kids, whether it's your spouse, and not really honoring any pleasure for yourself. So making that time and that space to care for yourself, even if it's just taking a bath or a shower on your own or going to the bathroom on your own when you have small children, can be like a feat in itself. What can you do that feels pleasurable? And I don't just mean like getting it out of the house to go grocery shopping on your own. I think sometimes even our spouses, they don't get it. They're like, I just let you go to the store on your own. And I'm like, "No, like that's not, you know, going to Trader Joe's isn't exactly my choice for a pleasurable activity. I would like, you know, a massage or to get a pedicure or to just take a bath by myself. Are there other ways that we can engage in pleasure?" Um, so I think that's a good touch point as well as intimacy beyond sexual intimacy, right? talking with your partner, connecting on an emotional level, having a date night, even if it's inside the house after the kids are in bed, for you to actually connect with each other and not just talk about, okay, what do we do now? Who needs a diaper change? What kid needs to get signed up for swim lessons? You know, things besides the logistics, actual communicating and intimacy about what makes you and your husband feel close and partner. That's That's amazing. That's amazing. Yeah, good advice. Good advice, Holly. I love that. So, how can partners show up in a way that supports connection without pressure or expectation, especially during those early years with the little ones? I think prioritizing time for connection, and I specifically use the phrase of connection. I think scheduling sex specifically can be helpful for some people, right? I think a lot of times it gets a bad rep as being unsexy, but also it's a way to show that it's a priority of yours. Kind of like if you buy tickets to a concert in advance, it doesn't make the concert any less exciting. However, it can also cause pressure. It's like, "Oh my gosh, Saturday night's coming up. This is the night that we have sex." And that can add too much pressure. So, I think a good way to turn the pressure down is just focus on having connection time. Again, is that sitting on the couch watching one of your favorite movies and sipping hot chocolate together? Is it cuddling in bed? Is it taking a shower together, massaging each other? Right. Different ways to engage in connection without the expectation of sex. I love that. I love that. I My husband and I, it's one of the things we do. We hop on the couch usually around 9:00 when you know when it quiets down we'll hop on the couch and he always hands me his feet and that's sort of like I rub his feet and it's just sort of one way that makes him know like we're I'm we're still connected. I I've got you. It's okay, you know. And sometimes I'll hand him my feet and make him be reciprocal, but most of the time it's it's it's just me rubbing his feet most of the time. That's okay. >> Oh, I love it. [laughter] >> Yeah. For me, it's like the one of like the most intimate connecting things that my partner does is when he washes my back with the lofah in the shower. It just like is so nice to have like somebody else wash her back and I can like relax and just enjoy being in the shower together. >> Oh, it's that's so sweet. I love that. We used to shower a lot more together, honestly. You know, when we only had three kids in the house. [laughter] >> Only three. Now that there are five kids in the house, like we rarely, you know, it's busy. It really is. It really does take a lot of management just to figure out how to meet everybody's needs kind of. And you know, meeting my needs is one of the things we got to meet along the way, too. Definitely. That is a priority for me now in a bigger way than it ever has been in my life before. I think it's cuz I have so many other people to take care of. I know if I don't take care of myself, I'm not going to be able to take care of anyone else. >> Right. Yeah. How are you going to do it if you're so empty and depleted? How are you going to be able to show up for all these people that you care about? >> Yeah, absolutely. So, you've said before that pleasure is a form of self-care. I love that. What does that really mean in practice for women who are balancing their careers and families and everything else? Oh, goodness. It looks like time management.
